description: Policing, courts, incarceration, probation, re-entry. The system that touches more American lives than any other — and destroys more. Restorative justice as the alternative architecture.

dome_relevance.1: Legal — criminal law, rights, due process, collateral consequences
dome_relevance.2: Systems — courts, jails, probation offices, bail systems
dome_relevance.6: Economic — cost of incarceration, employment barriers with records

sphere_relevance.1: Parcel — property seized through civil forfeiture
sphere_relevance.9: Policy — sentencing reform, bail reform, police oversight

adjacent_disciplines.description: Healing harm through dialogue, accountability, community process
Supporting people returning from incarceration — housing, jobs, identity documents

practitioners.name: Bryan Stevenson
practitioners.practice: Death penalty defense, racial justice litigation, narrative memorialization
practitioners.approach: Each of us is more than the worst thing we've ever done. The system works by dehumanizing — turning people into cases, numbers, sentences. Justice requires proximity: you must be close enough to the condemned to see them as human. Mercy is not weakness — it is the measure of our justice.
practitioners.body_of_work: Founded Equal Justice Initiative. Won Supreme Court rulings banning mandatory life-without-parole for children. Just Mercy (book and film). National Memorial for Peace and Justice — the first memorial to lynching victims. Freed over 140 wrongly condemned prisoners.
practitioners.why_instructive: The dome's legal layer must reckon with the criminal justice system's ability to destroy every other layer simultaneously — an arrest collapses housing, employment, health, community, education. Stevenson shows what repair looks like.

dome_relevance.1: Legal — criminal defense, wrongful conviction, death penalty abolition
dome_relevance.4: Health — mental health in incarceration, trauma of the condemned
dome_relevance.6: Economic — employment barriers, economic destruction of conviction
dome_relevance.8: Community — community impact of mass incarceration

sphere_relevance.5: Social — the communities mass incarceration devastates
sphere_relevance.6: Temporal — lynching memorial makes invisible history visible in physical space
sphere_relevance.9: Policy — sentencing reform, juvenile justice reform

practitioners.name: Jean Valjean
practitioners.creator: Victor Hugo
practitioners.practice: Re-entry, identity transformation, running from the system while building community
practitioners.approach: A man steals bread for his sister's children. Serves 19 years. Is given a second chance by a bishop's mercy. Spends the rest of his life trying to be worthy of that mercy while the system (Javert) insists he can never change. His question: can a convicted person become a new person?
practitioners.body_of_work: Transforms from prisoner to factory owner to mayor to father figure. Builds a factory that employs an entire town. Saves lives at the barricade. The entire novel is about whether the dome — the full system — can recognize a person's transformation or only their worst moment.
practitioners.why_instructive: He is the re-entry case study. Every dome layer either helps or hunts him. The legal layer (Javert) says he can never change. The community layer (Fantine, Cosette) shows he already has. The dome must be more like the bishop than Javert.

dome_relevance.1: Legal — parole, identity documents, criminal record as permanent barrier
dome_relevance.2: Systems — the pursuit that never stops (Javert as system)
dome_relevance.3: Fiscal — building economic capacity from nothing
dome_relevance.6: Economic — from convict to factory owner to mayor
dome_relevance.8: Community — building community while hiding from the system
dome_relevance.10: Autonomy — the right to become someone new
dome_relevance.12: Flourishing — redemption, mercy, love as what flourishing looks like

sphere_relevance.5: Social — how a community receives or rejects someone with a record
sphere_relevance.6: Temporal — 19 years of incarceration, decades of running, a lifetime of transformation

practitioners.name: Danielle Sered
practitioners.practice: Restorative justice, violence intervention, survivor-centered accountability
practitioners.approach: Incarceration does not make survivors safer. Common Justice runs an alternative-to-incarceration program where the person who caused harm meets with the person they harmed. The survivor defines what accountability looks like. Most survivors choose this over prison.
practitioners.body_of_work: Founded Common Justice — the first alternative-to-incarceration program for violent felonies in the US. Until We Reckon (2019). Shows that when you ask survivors of violence what they want, most don't want prison — they want safety, accountability, and answers.
practitioners.why_instructive: She proves that the dome's legal layer doesn't have to mean incarceration. Accountability without cages. Safety without surveillance. The dome must offer this path.

dome_relevance.1: Legal — alternative to incarceration for violent felonies
dome_relevance.4: Health — trauma healing for both survivors and those who caused harm
dome_relevance.8: Community — restorative process rebuilds community trust

sphere_relevance.5: Social — community-based accountability changes neighborhood dynamics
sphere_relevance.9: Policy — proves viable alternatives to incarceration at scale
